question: Explain the purpose and typical use cases of the kill() function in UNIX system programming.
expectedPoints: The kill() function is used to send a signal to a process or a group of processes.
Its primary purpose is to request the termination of a process, but it can send any signal defined in the system.
Common use cases include terminating a process (SIGTERM, SIGKILL), stopping/resuming a process (SIGSTOP, SIGCONT), or notifying a process of an event (SIGHUP, SIGUSR1).
The function takes a process ID (PID) and a signal number as arguments. A PID of 0 sends the signal to all processes in the sender's process group.

question: Describe how the raise() function differs from the kill() function. Provide a simple code example to illustrate the use of raise().
expectedPoints: The raise() function sends a signal to the calling process itself, whereas kill() can send a signal to any process (subject to permissions).
raise(sig) is essentially equivalent to kill(getpid(), sig).
It is used when a process needs to signal itself, often to trigger a signal handler for error handling or self-management.
Example code: `if (error_condition) { raise(SIGUSR1); }` to signal an error condition to itself.

question: Discuss the security implications and permission checks involved when using the kill() function to send a signal to another process.
expectedPoints: A process must have the appropriate permissions to send a signal to another process. The effective user ID of the sender must match the real or effective user ID of the target process, unless the sender has root privileges.
Root privileges (superuser) can send any signal to any process.
The kill() function will fail with EPERM if the sender lacks permissions, or with ESRCH if the target process does not exist.
This permission system prevents users from arbitrarily interrupting or terminating each other's processes, ensuring a level of process isolation and system security.
